[174:56]It was just today that Iran's foreign minister said, we're not going to negotiate over our

[175:01]nuclear program anymore.

[175:05]What do you think the strategic calculus is in Israel?

[175:14]Since that war, Iran has expelled all of the inspectors.

[175:18]They've turned off all the cameras in Iran's nuclear facilities.

[175:22]They've made it clear that Israel and the United States did not destroy all of their

[175:27]centrifuges, did not destroy their stockpile of enriched uranium.

[175:31]As a matter of fact, Iran says we're not even aware of all of their nuclear facilities

[175:36]and the extent of their nuclear program.

[175:42]that they have closed the door to any future negotiations. So what is the

[175:49]strategic thinking of Israel? If all of that has taken place, and it has been

[175:54]four months since Israel and the United States went to war with Iran, then the

[175:59]Israelis must assume that in this time period, Iran has been rearming

[176:11]They have gotten more weapons systems from Russia, air defense systems, maybe Air Force.

[176:19]They're getting stronger. They're rooting out Mossad operatives that have infiltrated their country.

[176:25]And if Iran has gotten stronger, you would have to argue that Israel must assume

[176:30]that Iran is closer to a bomb now than they were four months ago

[176:34]in the immediate aftermath of the strikes,

[176:41]which is that we're overdue for another Israel Iran war. And could that be the reason why

[176:48]Israel has stalled the fighting in Gaza, temporary ceasefire so they could prepare to challenge

[176:55]Iran all over again? To me, those are the reasons that we have the ceasefire. And so in other

[177:05]to denuclearize Iran without toppling the government.

[177:10]That is technically America's strategic objective.

[177:13]Maybe that's not my idea of what our foreign policy should be,

[177:17]but that is logically,

[177:18]and that is what our government has promulgated,

[177:21]that is our strategic doctrine.

[177:24]Denuclearize Iran through diplomacy

[177:26]without toppling the government.

[177:28]And in Gaza, our objective is to end the war.

[177:31]and make sure that Israel does not annex the territory while also making sure that Hamas does not come back into power.

[177:39]That's our strategic objective.

[177:42]And so we are trying our best, America's trying our best to make sure that Israel backs off of Iran and backs off of Gaza,

[177:50]and we're trying to get them to agree to hold themselves back.

[177:55]But if you understand Israel's strategic objectives, you understand that this is impossible.

[178:01]It's only a matter of time before one day they go back into Gaza, one day they go back

[178:06]into Iran.

[178:07]And the reason they have agreed tenuously to these peace agreements is because it's

[178:13]part of a broader strategic timeline, which is that eventually once they've regrouped

[178:18]and reorganized, they're going to take the fight to another country.

[178:21]And then once they're done with that, they're going to go back into Gaza.

[178:28]They'll go back against Iran and they'll either finish the job or they'll stop, get a ceasefire

[178:33]and then go back in a Gaza and they'll either finish the job there or they'll get another

[178:39]ceasefire, take the fight somewhere else, regroup.

[178:43]This has been the cycle for two years.

[178:45]In some ways it's been the cycle for 70 years.

[178:49]This is how they operate. So I, I think it's really telling. It has not been two weeks

[178:55]since the deal came through and Trump had this big ceremony with all the world leaders

[179:02]talking about a Nobel prize, his triumph front page of time magazine on the cover. And the

[179:09]deal is already falling apart. It's just been two weeks.

[179:12]And the deal is already at risk of collapsing.

[179:15]They're sending over a high power delegation to oversee the implementation because everybody

[179:22]knows it's not going to hold.

[179:25]And what an embarrassment that'll be for this administration.

[179:28]And when the ceasefire collapses, I hope that proves once and for all for people that only

[179:38]If Trump is begging our own ally that we pay, please come to the table and make a deal.

[179:44]And they continually make an ass out of him by leading him on, signing on to deals they

[179:51]never intend to follow through with.

[180:00]So, that's that.

[180:01]But I do want to move on.

[180:02]I want to get into Russia.

[180:06]We spent a lot of time talking about Gaza, and we've been at it already for an hour.

[180:15]There's some other big developments in the war in Ukraine.

[180:19]And here we go again.

[180:44]to take back all of Ukraine. We're going to give them Tomahawk missiles. We're done playing

[184:36]That Trump has authorized Ukraine to strike inside Russian territory and that Trump is now putting sanctions on Russia's energy companies.

[189:34]to win. They still think they have some card to play here. They still think they have leverage.

[189:50]over the situation. Kiev does not, Paris does not, London does not, Berlin does not.

[190:23]They've been struggling for example in Pocrovsk. I think I'm saying that right

[190:27]Which is a major city they fought for for years. They control half of it now. They might at any moment break through

[191:32]And why are they saying that? It's obvious. They know Ukraine has no ability to counterattack.

[191:38]There's going to be no more counteroffensives. There's going to be no more counterattacks.

[191:47]So why have the demands changed? They used to be so bold saying we're literally going to push the Russians back

[191:55]We're gonna mouth this counteroffensive the spring counteroffensive and we're a shove them back over the border

[192:05]Just stop the Russian advance freeze a line of contact and the reason they're saying that is because they want to rearm

[192:13]they want to give Ukraine the opportunity to

[192:21]They want to mobilize more men, train more men.

[192:26]They want to rebuild their defensive line.

[192:49]they want to build another trench.

[192:52]And Russia knows that.

[192:53]That's why Russia is not agreeing to a temporary ceasefire, because they know that if there's

[193:08]So, this is the state of the play.

[193:11]Now, the big concern among the Ukrainians, why will they not agree to give up this territory?

[193:29]that Russia might restart the war.

[193:43]they're worried that Russia without a NATO commitment is going to start the war all over

[193:48]again to take more territory. Both sides will get a reprieve. Russia will get sanctions relief.

[194:01]They'll be able to rebuild a little bit on their side. And then who knows, in a long

[194:06]enough timeline, in years, maybe a decade, Russia will restart the advance and they'll

[194:10]be back to take more territory. They'll have the element of surprise. So what Ukraine wants

[194:16]are security guarantees. Well, how do you finesse this? That's sort of the whole point.

[194:23]Russia wants Ukraine out of NATO. If Ukraine agrees not to be a NATO, they're saying, well,

[194:28]we want a security commitment. Well, that's what NATO is. NATO is a security commitment.

[194:33]So how do you give Ukraine a NATO-like security commitment without giving them NATO and effectively

[194:40]that form of a security commitment? So there's all kinds of creative solutions that are being

[194:46]put out there, like for example, that the United States will take a stake in Ukraine's

[194:51]minerals and energy resources, because if Americans are put in the way of the Russian

[194:57]military, that serves as an unofficial tripwire. Russia cannot restart the war without killing

[195:08]security guarantee. You don't have to put American soldiers and bases and equipment.

[195:15]almost like a human shield. And Russia knows that if they invade, they're attacking an

[195:21]American economic partner. Maybe Ukraine's in the European Union. It's like a soft guarantee

[195:26]that doesn't involve a ton of American security personnel and equipment on the border. That

[195:32]might be an effective tripwire. But this is not good enough for the Ukrainians and the

[195:37]Europeans. They're saying that's not going to do anything. Russia might invade anyway.

[195:44]a defensive pact. And Europe wants America to bring in a defensive pact. But here's

[195:50]what's funny about this. America doesn't want to do this for Ukraine. Europe doesn't

[195:57]even want to do this for Ukraine. This is what Kiev wants. Kiev wants security.

[200:08]regime change in Venezuela. We're doing more sanctions on Russia than Biden. We're doing

[200:14]missile strikes on Russia that were authorized under Biden. The peace in Gaza is falling

[200:20]apart. We already bombed Iran and many ways World War three is inevitable unless you get

[200:27]an administration that is truly America first, that is actually willing to give up actually

[200:38]the biggest and broadest claims of American empire. Maybe it's time to give up Ukraine.

[200:43]Maybe it's time actually to give up on some of these things. Maybe it's time to recognize

[200:48]that some of these commitments are not actually doing anything for America. We have overcommitted

[200:54]and let's just be honest with ourselves. People talk about projecting strength. What strength?

[201:01]Look at our supply chains. Look at our insolvency crisis. Look at our, look at our own cities.

[201:08]We have a very serious problem, which is that people are talking about, we need to project

[201:14]strength abroad. First of all, we're overcommitted. That's to begin with. We're committed to defending

[201:27]We're trying to overturn all of the governments in Central and South America, and we're doing

[201:34]it from a very weak position.

[201:36]The commitments are increasing, but our ability to finance them is decreasing.

[201:42]The supply chains that create the productive capacity for our wealth, for our power projection

[201:51]is in jeopardy.

[201:53]China is putting restrictions on rare earths. We have this enormous appetite for electricity,

[202:00]for silicon wafers, for chips. The cities are exploding with political violence, racial

[202:06]violence. We have a $1 trillion interest payment every year. We have a $30 trillion debt.

[202:18]If there's going to be a way out of this,

[202:20]we are going to actually have to do a managed retreat.

[202:24]And you can call it whatever you want.

[202:26]You can call it something else.

[202:28]But if we don't do something like that,

[202:31]there's gonna be a collapse.

[202:33]And by that, I don't mean it's gonna be like

[202:35]post-apocalyptic wasteland, although it might be.

[202:39]But I'm saying that something has gotta give here.

[202:42]We actually can't defend Taiwan, Ukraine, and Israel simultaneously while we're at war with Venezuela,

[202:50]while there's major civil instability in the cities, while our supply chains are in jeopardy,

[202:56]China's doing all the rare earths processing, Taiwan makes all the advanced semiconductors and chips,

[203:03]we can't pay for anything, we have inflation, we have these economic problems,

[203:08]People talk about projecting power.

[203:11]There's gonna be no more power

[203:12]if we don't get our own house in order.

[203:15]And if all of our commitments to every country,

[203:17]if those commitments are tested,

[203:19]the whole thing is gonna collapse like a house of cards.

[203:22]Because right now, our ability to defend

[203:24]these other countries is theoretical.

[203:27]Could we win a war with China, Russia, Iran,

[203:30]North Korea, and Venezuela simultaneously?

[203:32]That's all theoretical.

[203:35]We could maybe defend Taiwan from China. We could maybe defend Ukraine from Russia.

[203:40]And so on. Can we do them all simultaneously for a prolonged period of time? Probably not.

[203:49]If that were tested, if those commitments were put to the test,

[203:53]probably you'd find out very quickly that we could not do those things.

[203:58]And we would be undermined from within. So

[204:03]This is a real problem. And I think the Trump is making it worse because Trump is not who

[204:08]he said he was. These commitments that he made, we're going to end all the wars, no

[204:13]new wars, et cetera. It wasn't true. America first bringing people that are against the

[204:18]deep state never happened.

[204:21]So

[204:23]We're in a lot of trouble here. This is not, this does not feel like a good situation.

[204:28]And I would urge a lot of people that are delusional about this, people that are, you

[204:32]know, these faggots online that are talking about, don't chimp out. First of all, you're

[204:37]fucking 40 years old. That's not funny. People say, we shouldn't chimp. Don't chimp out

[204:42]on Trump. Trust the plan. First of all, that's not funny. Those are stale memes. You're 37

[204:55]People that are on Twitter.

[204:57]It's not about chipping out or trusting the plan.

[204:59]We have to stop being delusional and in denial, we are headed towards serious problems.

[205:06]Anybody that has their finger on the pulse can see this.

[205:15]that we are headed towards a seismic inflection point.

[205:19]We're talking 1917, 1945, 1989, all rolled into one.
